course_description:
This course involves a masterclass presented by a leading UQ researcher and/or Visiting Scholar with research interests in Western Civilisation. This course aims to bring Honours students into direct contact with cutting-edge research and the masterclass will involve readings relating to either a current project or specialist research interest of a leading researcher. It will provide an opportunity to learn directly how this researcher designs and conducts their research program.

Among all the ancient civilizations, ancient Rome is perhaps the most familiar and well-known in ‘Western’ societies. Even now we often understand our own world and ourselves through Rome. This course explores key responses to ancient Rome across diverse media both high and popular (ballet, opera, tragedy and comedy, the novel, film, television and the visual arts). Through these works, we explore: why ancient Rome has mattered to later societies; which elements of ancient Rome have been most usable; how later cultures have made use of them; and what determines the way ancient Rome is represented and consumed.

restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the BAdvHum(Hons) Western Civilisation Field of Study

course_aims: The aim of WCIV6000 is to introduce students to and involve them in cutting-edge research projects that address issues in the study of Western Civilisation. Students should come to understand the process of research project design and execution through participation, discussion and independent research. They should learn to evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of different methodological approaches to a research question, understand that different research questions require different methods and theories, and engage with other researchers to advance their own research skills.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate, through oral participation and written communication, knowledge of cutting-edge research projects that address issues in the study of Western Civilisation.
Understand the process of research project design and execution.
Evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of different methodological approaches to a research question.
Understand that different research questions require different methods and theories.
Develop skills in group work and oral presentations.

assessment_details.title: Participation
assessment_details.task_description: Participation is essential for passing this course, and requires you to interact respectfully and learn from your peers. Students are expected to participate in seminar discussions and relevant seminar tasks. This assessment requires you to show evidence of preparation for seminars and participate in small group discussions with relevant and well-focused comments. assessment_details.title: Seminar Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Dates for presentations will be allocated at the first class session.
assessment_details.task_description: At the start of the semester, students will select one of the above works of reception on which to give an introductory ten-minute presentation. The presentation should introduce the background of the work in Roman historical sources, the modern context of its production, and its key features of interest. The presentation should be accompanied by a powerpoint. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Research Essay
assessment_details.task_description: The topic of your essay will be developed in consultation with the course co-ordinator. Basic bibliography will be provided; however, students are expected to go beyond it in researching their topic. The essay should be between 2,000-2,500 words. In addition to the submission of an essay, students will have a viva voce examination on their essay's content and research methodology. These will be scheduled following submission of the essay. The viva will be used to confirm grades awarded on the written assessment. Failure of the viva will result in the essay being awarded a failing grade. Further details in relation to the viva will be provided on the course website. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
